burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery Rochester NY per FA

notable.notable_blurb: First Judge of Monroe County, NY; Yale 1809

education.notes: Prepared for college by Nathan Johnson. Yale class of 1809.
Attended Litchfield Law School 1810; thereafter read law in Canandaigua NY.

career.notes: Partner with William H. Adams. Admitted to bar 1812.
Purchased 1,000 acres near Rochester with Elisha Beach; developed Carthage area; erected storehouses, incline railway, bridge over lower falls.
Elected from Ontario County.
Appointed March 5, 1821 by Governor DeWitt Clinton. Monroe County organized February 23, 1821.
Moved to Detroit 1861; died there Oct 14, 1867.

institutions.institution_blurb: Attended 1810; appointed first judge of Monroe County NY by 1821.

cross_connections.display_label: Both Strong men who married Hooker descendants, 84 years apart

narrative_blocks.header: First Judge of Monroe County, appointed 1821
narrative_blocks.body: Elisha Beebe Strong was appointed the first judge of Monroe County, New York on March 5, 1821 by Governor DeWitt Clinton — just days after the county itself was organized by the state legislature from Ontario County territory. A Yale graduate (1809) and Litchfield Law School alumnus, he had also served in the New York State Assembly in 1819-1820 and as a presidential elector in 1824, becoming one of the most prominent legal figures in upstate New York.

narrative_blocks.header: His Hooker marriage repeated a Strong family tradition
narrative_blocks.body: When Elisha Beebe Strong married Dolly Goodwin Hooker in 1813 at Canandaigua, New York, he was not the first Strong to wed a Thomas Hooker descendant: 84 years earlier, his ancestral kin Captain Asahel Strong Jr. had married Sarah Hooker of Farmington, Connecticut in 1729, producing a line that named a grandson 'Asahel Hooker Strong' to preserve the memory. The two unions — Farmington 1729, Canandaigua 1813 — trace the same Strong-Hooker affinity across the Great Migration and the early Republic, from a Farmington lawyer's household to a frontier judge's courtroom on the Erie Canal.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
FindAGrave

narrative_blocks.body: Elisha Beebe Strong was appointed the first judge of Monroe County, New York on March 5, 1821 by Governor DeWitt Clinton — just days after the county itself was organized by the state legislature from Ontario County territory. A Yale graduate (1809) and Litchfield Law School alumnus, he had also served in the New York State Assembly in 1819-1820 and as a presidential elector in 1824, becoming one of the most prominent legal figures in upstate New York.

narrative_blocks.header: His Hooker marriage repeated a Strong family tradition
narrative_blocks.body: When Elisha Beebe Strong married Dolly Goodwin Hooker in 1813 at Canandaigua, New York, he was not the first Strong to wed a Thomas Hooker descendant: 84 years earlier, his ancestral kin Captain Asahel Strong Jr. had married Sarah Hooker of Farmington, Connecticut in 1729, producing a line that named a grandson 'Asahel Hooker Strong' to preserve the memory. The two unions — Farmington 1729, Canandaigua 1813 — trace the same Strong-Hooker affinity across the Great Migration and the early Republic, from a Farmington lawyer's household to a frontier judge's courtroom on the Erie Canal.

cross_connections.display_label: Hooker women who married Strongs in 1729 and 1813

narrative_blocks.header: Her husband was Monroe County's first judge
narrative_blocks.body: Dolly Goodwin Hooker married Elisha Beebe Strong, who in 1821 became the first judge of Monroe County, New York. She died in Rochester in 1850; he remarried and moved to Detroit, dying there in 1867.

narrative_blocks.header: Her Hooker-Strong marriage echoed one from 1729
narrative_blocks.body: When Dolly Goodwin Hooker married Elisha Beebe Strong in 1813, she entered a family that had already wed into the Hooker line 84 years before: Captain Asahel Strong Jr. had married her distant cousin Sarah Hooker at Farmington in 1729, and a grandson of that marriage — Asahel Hooker Strong — was already carrying her maiden name as his own middle name by 1776. Dolly's marriage completed the second arc; her great-grandson Beebe Hooker Strong, born in Milwaukee in 1866, would carry the name to a third generation on the Wisconsin frontier.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery Rochester NY per FA

bio.photo_notes: Rear Admiral James Hooker Strong, USN (1814-1882)

burial.plot_notes: Green-Wood Cemetery Brooklyn per FA/Wikipedia

notable.notable_blurb: Rear Admiral; Battle of Mobile Bay hero, 1864

education.notes: Interrupted studies Feb 2, 1829 to accept naval appointment as midshipman.

career.notes: Full career: Midshipman Feb 2, 1829; Passed Midshipman Jun 4, 1836; Lieutenant Sep 1841; Commander Apr 24, 1861; Captain Aug 5, 1865; Commodore Mar 21, 1870; Rear Admiral Sep 1873; retired Apr 25, 1876 after 47 years service.

military_service.unit: USS Mohawk, USS Flag (South Atlantic Blockading Squadron); USS Monongahela (West Gulf Blockading Squadron)
military_service.notes: May 8, 1862: captured schooner Amelia off Charleston aboard USS Flag (blockade runner with cotton). Aug 5, 1864: Battle of Mobile Bay — commanding USS Monongahela, rammed Confederate ironclad CSS Tennessee to shield Farragut flagship USS Hartford; promoted to Captain for this action. Commanded USS Canandaigua in Mediterranean 1869-70. Commanded South Atlantic Squadron as Rear Admiral 1873-75. Retired Apr 25, 1876.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' honors the maternal Hooker lineage.

cross_connections.display_label: Both fought at Mobile Bay under Admiral Farragut, 1864

narrative_blocks.header: Rammed the Confederate ironclad to shield Admiral Farragut
narrative_blocks.body: Commanding USS Monongahela at the Battle of Mobile Bay on August 5, 1864, James Hooker Strong drove his ship into the Confederate ironclad CSS Tennessee to protect Admiral Farragut's flagship USS Hartford from a ramming attack — exposing Monongahela to point-blank broadsides that destroyed her iron cutwater. His initiative contributed to Tennessee's surrender after ninety minutes of combat, earned him promotion to captain, and stands as the defining action of the last great naval battle of the Civil War.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Navy destroyers later bore his name
narrative_blocks.body: Two United States Navy ships bore the name USS Strong in honor of Rear Admiral James Hooker Strong: DD-467, a Fletcher-class destroyer commissioned in 1943 and sunk by Japanese aircraft off Bougainville in the Solomon Islands in November 1943; and DD-758, later sold to Brazil in 1973 as the Rio Grande del Norte, which served through the Korean and Vietnam Wars before being sunk in a storm off Durban, South Africa in 1997 while under tow to the scrapyard. His middle name 'Hooker' was a deliberate genealogical honor — his mother Dolly Goodwin Hooker made him one of the most celebrated figures to carry the Hooker family name.

narrative_blocks.header: Enrolled at the school his uncle later led
narrative_blocks.body: James Hooker Strong enrolled at the Polytechnic College in Chittenango, New York around 1827 before his 1829 naval appointment — the same institution where his aunt's husband, Reverend Andrew Yates (who had married Hannah Allin Hooker, sister of Strong's mother Dolly), would later serve as principal. The Hooker family's deep connections to the Schenectady–Chittenango educational corridor shaped Strong's early formation, and the Polytechnic College's technical emphasis in mathematics and applied science gave him the precise preparation the Navy sought in its post-War of 1812 officer cohort.

narrative_blocks.header: His aunt's husband's grandson fought the same battle
narrative_blocks.body: Captain Arthur Reed Yates — grandson of Reverend Andrew Yates, who had married Strong's aunt Hannah Allin Hooker — served on Admiral Farragut's personal staff at the very same Battle of Mobile Bay on August 5, 1864, earning Farragut's written commendation for gallantry; he died in 1892 on the very eve of promotion to Commodore. Two men bound by the same Schenectady household — one commanding a ship, the other on the admiral's staff — were both at the decisive moment of the last great naval engagement of the Civil War.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery Rochester NY per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Bank cashier at Batavia; dead at twenty-two
narrative_blocks.body: Julian Elisha Strong, named for his mother's family (the Elisha Beebe Strongs had a tradition of honoring elder kin), became a bank cashier at Batavia, New York — a modest career cut short when he died on September 15, 1838, at age twenty-two, unmarried. His name lived on: his sister Martha Cornelia named her son Julien Elisha Winder in his honor, a generational echo that survived him by 75 years.

narrative_blocks.header: Bertha, her twin, died young at twenty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: Martha Cornelia Strong was born as a twin with her sister Bertha Rosabella on April 13, 1818 in Rochester, New York. Bertha died in 1846 at age twenty-eight; Martha survived as a starred entry in the Hooker genealogy.

career.role: Clerk of Supreme Court of Territory of Michigan

career.notes: On staffs of many Michigan governors; member of the Brady Guards of Detroit.

narrative_blocks.header: Michigan militia colonel who lived to ninety-two
narrative_blocks.body: John Winder served as City Clerk of Detroit from 1832 to 1836, Clerk of the Circuit Court from 1848 to 1870, and as a colonel on the staffs of numerous Michigan governors — a civic career spanning over four decades in the early American Midwest. He died in Detroit on September 26, 1897 at the extraordinary age of ninety-two, surviving his wife Martha Cornelia Strong by nearly three years.

burial.plot_notes: Elmwood Cemetery Detroit per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Died at twenty-two, months after first childbirth
narrative_blocks.body: Catherine 'Kittie' Granger Winder Chase died February 11, 1860 in Wayne County Michigan at age twenty-two — approximately two and a half months after the birth of her only son John Winder Chase on November 26, 1859. The compressed birth-then-death timeline strongly suggests childbirth complications or postpartum illness. Her son John grew up motherless from infancy.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Marcus Chase 1857, son John born 1859
narrative_blocks.body: Kittie married Marcus A. Chase in 1857 in Wayne County Michigan. Their only documented child John Winder Chase was born November 26, 1859 in Detroit — the boy carried his mother's maiden name Winder forward as his middle name, preserving the Winder family line into the next generation. Marcus survived her by thirty years, dying in 1890.

narrative_blocks.header: Michigan 1838-1860; buried Elmwood Cemetery Detroit
narrative_blocks.body: Catherine Granger Winder was born January 1, 1838 in Michigan and died February 11, 1860 in Wayne County Michigan at age twenty-two. She is buried at Elmwood Cemetery in Detroit — the major nineteenth-century Detroit garden cemetery. Her father Colonel John Winder and mother Martha Cornelia Strong Winder placed her among the prominent Detroit-Michigan Winder-Strong-Hooker family network of the mid-nineteenth century.

burial.plot_notes: Forest Home Cemetery Milwaukee WI per FA

career.notes: Served during winter 1839-40.

career.organization: Kellogg & Strong Steamboat Company and Warehouse, Milwaukee
career.notes: Prominent Milwaukee merchant.

narrative_blocks.header: Helped secure the land for Forest Home Cemetery
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Hunter Strong was instrumental in securing the land for Forest Home Cemetery in Milwaukee and persuading St. Paul's Episcopal Church to establish the cemetery there — a civic contribution that anchored Milwaukee's most prominent burial ground. He came to Wisconsin around 1830, clerked in the territorial legislature in 1839-40, built his fortune through the Kellogg & Strong Steamboat company and Newberry Furnace, and was twice married.

narrative_blocks.header: Bridged two Strong-Hooker marriages and two frontier states
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Hunter Strong was the son of the second Strong-Hooker marriage — Judge Elisha Beebe Strong and Dolly Goodwin Hooker — and the father of the man who would carry that name to a third generation: Beebe Hooker Strong, born Milwaukee 1866. He came to Wisconsin around 1830 at age ten, making the journey from the Rochester land grants his father had built to the Great Lakes frontier, and with him the Strong-Hooker legacy crossed into a new American West.

burial.plot_notes: Forest Home Cemetery Milwaukee per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived her Milwaukee pioneer husband by nineteen years
narrative_blocks.body: Rosa Searls Warner Strong, widowed when Robert Hunter Strong died at Waukesha in May 1892, lived on for nineteen more years — dying in Chicago on July 14, 1911 at age 87 while visiting her daughter, having outlasted her husband long enough to see her children established across three states. Her obituary noted that Robert had been instrumental in founding Forest Home Cemetery and that the family had been prominent at St. Paul's Episcopal Church — the church whose cemetery he helped build would ultimately receive them both.

burial.plot_notes: Valhalla Memorial Park Milwaukee per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Niece of an admiral; her husband held Helena
narrative_blocks.body: Bertha Luceba Strong Carlson was the niece of Rear Admiral James Hooker Strong — the officer who rammed the Confederate ironclad CSS Tennessee at Mobile Bay — and the wife of Dr. Oscar Wilhelm Carlson, a Stockholm-born physician who had survived a shipwreck at age ten, fought in a 4,000-vs-18,000 battle at Helena, Arkansas, and later became president of the Wisconsin Homeopathic Society. Her family gathered the military heroism and the immigrant drive of mid-nineteenth century America into a single Milwaukee household.

career.organization: St. Paul Pioneer Press Dispatch, Saint Paul, Minnesota
career.notes: Makeup editor — responsible for typography, page layout, and press production. Nine years at the Pioneer Press Dispatch; fifty years in newspapers total.

narrative_blocks.header: Fifty years in newspapers, ending in Saint Paul
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Drake Strong spent fifty years in the newspaper trade, working as makeup editor of the St. Paul Pioneer Press Dispatch — a craft requiring command of typography, page layout, and production under deadline pressure, invisible to readers but essential to every edition. His career carried him from the Milwaukee frontier family of his father Robert Hunter Strong to the newspaper rooms of the Twin Cities, where the printed word had been his vocation since before Minnesota had achieved the scale of a major American press market.

bio.photo_notes: Beebe Hooker Strong (1866-1933), prominent Baraboo Wisconsin businessman and local historian

burial.plot_notes: Forest Home Cemetery Milwaukee per FA

career.organization: Baraboo Gas & Electric Light Company, Baraboo, Wisconsin
career.notes: W.H. Hopkins was president; B.H. Strong treasurer per period records.
Compiled and preserved historical accounts of the Baraboo region; documented account of how the Baraboo River received its name. Also managed local real estate.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' honors the maternal Hooker ancestry — his mother was Rosa Searls Warner Strong, wife of Robert Hunter Strong, whose mother Dolly Goodwin Hooker carried the Hooker surname directly.
First name 'Beebe' honors the Beebe family — his great-grandfather Judge Elisha BEEBE Strong (I00900) took the Beebe surname from his mother Mary Beebe Strong of Windsor, CT.

cross_connections.display_label: Both bore the Hooker name through the Strong family, 90 years apart

narrative_blocks.header: The name 'Hooker' traveled from Farmington to Milwaukee
narrative_blocks.body: Beebe Hooker Strong was born in Milwaukee in June 1866, bearing a middle name that traces a 137-year arc: his great-great-grandmother Sarah Hooker of Farmington, Connecticut had married into the Strong family in 1729, her great-great-grandson Asahel Hooker Strong preserved the name in Connecticut in 1776, and his grandmother Dolly Goodwin Hooker married a Strong again in 1813. The 'Hooker' in his name was a deliberate act of genealogical memory — a Wisconsin family's way of honoring the Hartford Puritan founder in whose blood they knew themselves to share.

narrative_blocks.header: Preserved how the Baraboo River got its name
narrative_blocks.body: Beebe Hooker Strong became a prominent civic figure in Baraboo, Wisconsin — Treasurer of the Baraboo Gas & Electric Light Company — where he compiled and preserved the documented account explaining how the Baraboo River received its unusual name, a piece of regional oral history that would otherwise have passed unrecorded. The same instinct that drove him to document local origins animated his family's naming practice: they called him 'Hooker' so that no descendant would forget where they came from, and he spent his career ensuring Baraboo didn't forget where it came from either.

burial.plot_notes: IOOF Cemetery Corvallis Benton Co OR — alongside husband Crosby George Davis and son Durrell Crosby Davis

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived her son; buried beside him in Oregon
narrative_blocks.body: Evelyn Lee Strong Davis buried her son Durrell Crosby Davis — killed at twenty-four by an accidental gunshot one day after the Armistice of 1918 — and was herself laid to rest at the IOOF Cemetery in Corvallis, Oregon nineteen years later, in the same ground as the husband who had predeceased her by thirteen years. She had come from a Milwaukee pioneer household, married in Blunt, South Dakota in 1886, raised her children across North Dakota and Oregon, and outlived nearly everyone.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' honors the maternal Hooker lineage.

narrative_blocks.header: Died young; daughter bequeathed to sister in Detroit
narrative_blocks.body: Eliza Hooker Strong Mumford died on June 5, 1844, at age twenty-one — just twenty months after the birth of her daughter Eliza Mumford — and by the terms of her will, bequeathed the child to her sister Martha Cornelia Strong Winder of Detroit, who 'adopted her as her own' under the name Eliza Mumford Winder. The child grew up among the Winder children of Detroit, married Charles B. Northrup in 1867, and carried both the Mumford and Winder names as testament to her mother's brief life.

burial.plot_notes: Woodmere Cemetery Detroit per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Orphaned at ten months; lived eighty-seven years
narrative_blocks.body: Eliza M. Mumford was born on August 23, 1843 in New York City, and her mother Eliza Hooker Strong died in Rochester in June 1844 when she was only ten months old — leaving her to be raised by her father Samuel Jones Mumford, who himself died in September 1850 when she was seven. She went on to outlive both parents by many decades, dying in Detroit on December 1, 1930 at age eighty-seven, having lived from Manhattan's antebellum world through the Civil War, the settling of the West, and the first World War.

burial.plot_notes: Crown Hill Cemetery Indianapolis per FA; died Kansas City MO Nov 1924

narrative_blocks.header: Born in 1830; outlived all her children
narrative_blocks.body: Henrietta Theresa Parsons Strong was born in Michigan in 1830 and died in Kansas City, Missouri in November 1924 at age ninety-three — surviving her husband George Parish Strong by thirty-seven years, the Civil War, two children who predeceased her, and the first World War, her life spanning virtually the entire arc of American expansion from the frontier Michigan of Andrew Jackson's presidency to the Model T era. She was buried at Crown Hill Cemetery in Indianapolis — the same cemetery that holds the tomb of President Benjamin Harrison — a woman who had watched three American generations unfold from a Michigan farm to a Missouri city, buried in a cemetery more famous for a president she had outlived.

sources.label: FamilySearch

burial.plot_notes: Green Mount Cemetery West Goshen Township Chester County per FA

career.organization: Connected with Lukens Steel, Coatesville, Pennsylvania
career.notes: Active member of Presbyterian Church; board of deacons. Per FA memorial.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' honors the family's Hooker ancestry — his grandmother Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong brought the Hooker name into the Strong family when she married Elisha Beebe Strong in 1813.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Hooker; his daughter married her Hooker cousin
narrative_blocks.body: George Hooker Strong was named for his grandmother Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong, who had brought the Hooker name into the Strong family through her 1813 marriage to Elisha Beebe Strong — and he carried that name west from Kansas, where he was born in 1871, to Pennsylvania, where he worked as an accountant connected to Lukens Steel in Coatesville and served on the board of deacons of the Presbyterian Church. He died in Altoona, Pennsylvania in 1938, outlived by a daughter and a grandson who carried the Hooker name into the Vietnam War.

narrative_blocks.header: An accountant connected with Lukens Steel in Pennsylvania
narrative_blocks.body: George Hooker Strong settled in Chester County, Pennsylvania, where he worked as an expert accountant connected with Lukens Steel — the Coatesville ironworks that had been rolling armor plate since the Civil War and would become one of the nation's most storied steel producers, known for the plates that built American warships and later for pioneering stainless steel production. He was an active member of the Presbyterian Church and served on the board of deacons, maintaining the denominational tradition of his grandfather George Parish Strong and the broader Hooker family's Congregationalist and Presbyterian roots.

narrative_blocks.header: His son Henry also bore the name Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: George Hooker Strong named his son Henry Hooker Strong (born 1901), carrying the name into yet another generation of the Strong family, four generations removed from Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong who first brought it into the Strongs through her 1813 marriage to Elisha Beebe Strong. Henry Hooker Strong's own son, Captain Henry Hooker Strong Jr., also bore the name — making it five consecutive generations of Hooker-named Strongs, ending with a Navy Skyhawk pilot shot down over North Vietnam in 1972.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
FindAGrave

death.cause: Heart failure on Monon train outside Indianapolis

burial.plot_notes: Rosehill Cemetery Chicago per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Died of heart failure on the Monon train
narrative_blocks.body: John Winder Strong, a newspaper man, died on January 3, 1904 when his heart failed on the Monon Railroad train outside Indianapolis at age fifty-two. He left behind a wife and two sons who would serve in two World Wars: Major General George Veazey Strong, who became the Army's intelligence chief in World War II, and Captain William Robert Strong, who fought at Chateau-Thierry and was killed in a hunting accident in 1919.

narrative_blocks.header: His sons fought in two separate World Wars
narrative_blocks.body: John Winder Strong had two sons who served in different American wars: his elder son George Veazey Strong became the Army's intelligence chief in World War II as a major general, and his younger son William Robert Strong fought at Chateau-Thierry in World War I as a captain before being killed in a Montana hunting accident in 1919, leaving a daughter born two months after his death. The newspaper man who died on the Monon train in 1904 left a military legacy that spanned two world wars.

sources.label: FindAGrave

burial.plot_notes: Crown Hill Cemetery Indianapolis per FA — same cemetery as his mother Henrietta Theresa Parsons Strong (I00958)

narrative_blocks.header: Buried at Crown Hill alongside his mother
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Hunter Strong died in Indianapolis on January 31, 1921, and was buried at Crown Hill Cemetery in Indianapolis — the same cemetery where his mother Henrietta Theresa Parsons Strong (who outlived him by three years, dying in 1924 at age ninety-three) was also interred, two members of the same family converging on the same Indiana cemetery after lives that had begun in Wisconsin and New York. He had been born on January 4, 1854 in Fond du Lac, Wisconsin — when his father George Parish Strong was still moving the family through the Midwest — and his wife Mary Elizabeth Johnson Strong died in Lafayette, Indiana in August 1915 at age forty-three, predeceasing him by nearly six years.

Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

INST024.name: Litchfield Law School
INST024.notes:
Founded 1784 by Judge Tapping Reeve (X00614) — the first law school in the United States. Tapping Reeve married Sarah "Sally" Burr Reeve (H00912), 5th-generation Hooker descendant, daughter of Esther Edwards Burr (H00378). Aaron Burr Jr. (H00913) — 5th-generation Hooker descendant — studied law here under his brother-in-law Tapping Reeve before enlisting in 1775. Among Litchfield's other notable students: John C. Calhoun (also became VP — two Vice Presidents produced by one law school). The school ran until 1833; at its peak enrolled students from across the country. Tapping Reeve House and Law School designated National Historic Landmark, 1965. In 1781, Reeve (with Theodore Sedgwick) represented Elizabeth Freeman ("Bett") in Brom & Bett v. Ashley — case that led to abolition of slavery in Massachusetts. Reeve also brought Rev. Lyman Beecher (father of Harriet Beecher Stowe) to Litchfield. The Litchfield Law School is among the most consequential institutions connected to the Hooker tree.
[hooker_connection] founded by in-law of Hooker descendant; Hooker descendant studied here

crossConnections.display_label: Both Strong men who married Hooker descendants, 84 years apart
